cognates.gloss: to cut, lop off; to pluck
cognates.correspondence_path: Ar ق ↔ Syc ܩ (*q); Ar ط ↔ Syc ܛ (*ṭ) — regular correspondence.
cognates.notes: The Arabic sense 'ever/never' (adverbial) is likely a semantic derivative of the verb meaning 'to cut/truncate' (i.e., 'up to this point in time').

cognates.correspondence_path: Ar ق ↔ He ק; Ar ط ↔ He ט; Ar final ط/ض ↔ He ע (common shift of *ḍ to ʕ/ע in Northwest Semitic).
cognates.notes: Semantic agreement is strong, but the Arabic root is geminate (III-ṭ), while Hebrew uses a third radical *ʕ. This may be a case where a Proto-Semitic biconsonantal base was expanded differently in different branches.

caveats: v0 prototype: this is an LLM-generated analysis, not a lexicon lookup. Results may be incomplete or incorrect, especially for Syriac, Tigrinya, and rare forms. Verify against Wiktionary or a scholarly lexicon before citing.
The Arabic lexeme 'قط' exists both as a verb meaning 'to cut' and as a particle (temporal adverb). This analysis mainly treats the root for the verbal sense.
The cognate relationship with Hebrew 'קטע' is speculative regarding the third radical; some scholars view these as distinct roots despite semantic similarity.
